comments.text: I believe this should say $R(T;k)\leq kn+O(1)$. For instance it is known for sufficiently large $n$ that $R(P_n; 3)=2n-1$ if $n$ is odd and $R(P_n;3)=2n-2$ if $n$ is even (Gy\'arf\'as, Ruszink\'o, S\'ark\"ozy, Szemer\'edi "Three-color Ramsey numbers for paths" 2007). On the other hand, for stars on $n$ vertices, we clearly have $R(K_{1,n-1};k)\geq kn-O(k)$ (and the exact result can be found in "On Ramsey numbers for stars" by Burr and Roberts (1973)). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Just to preemptively challenge my own comment, I suppose $R(T;k)=kn+O(1)$ means that there exists a constant $C$ (possibly depending on $k$) such that $R(T;k)\leq kn+C$. However, I can imagine somebody reading $R(T;k)=kn+O(1)$ and thinking it means that there exists constants $c$ and $C$ (possibly depending on $k$) such that $kn-c\leq R(T;k)\leq kn+C$. For what it's worth Erdős and Graham (in their 1973 paper) write this conjecture as $R(T;k)< kn+O(1)$.

Thanks! Which Erdős-Graham paper are you referring to? I can't find this reference. I also can't find this problem in [Er81c] either (I copied this reference from the graphs problem collection, and it looks like it's wrong there).

The paper is "On Partition Theorems for Finite Graphs" page 516 https://users.renyi.hu/~p_erdos/1975-23.pdf I see why I led you astray though. The paper says 1973 at the top, but I guess it was published in 1975.
